results.title: QuickQuill: Private, on-device meeting notes for Mac
results.content:
Subscribe

Launching today

QuickQuill

# QuickQuill

Launching today

## Private, on-device meeting notes for Mac

8 followers

## Private, on-device meeting notes for Mac

8 followers

Visit website

AI notetakers

•

Transcription

QuickQuill records your meetings and turns them into transcripts and summaries, and nothing is ever sent to the cloud. No bot joins the call, and it keeps working with Wi-Fi off, which is how the demo video was recorded. It also shows live subtitles with translation while you record, and there's push-to-talk dictation for any app. No account, no subscription. Dictation is free forever, and a single $49 purchase unlocks the full meeting workflow. Requires macOS 26 on Apple Silicon.

 Overview
 Reviews
 Alternatives
 Built with
 Team
 More

Free Options [...] Hi Product Hunt, I'm a solo developer in Japan👋 I built QuickQuill because I never got comfortable with meeting notetakers. You invite some company's bot into the call, it sits there in the participant list, everyone acts like that's normal, and the recording ends up on a server you never see. The apps say they won't look at it, and maybe they don't. I just didn't want work conversations to depend on that kind of trust. QuickQuill does everything on your Mac. It records both sides of the meeting, transcribes it, shows live subtitles with translation if you need them, and writes a summary when you stop. Nothing appears in the participant list because nothing joins the call. I recorded the demo on my M1 Mac with Wi-Fi turned off.  It requires macOS 26 on Apple Silicon. Dictation is free [...] Andras Czeizel

Recording the demo with Wi-Fi turned off is probably the best possible proof for this product :) I have never really liked meeting bots sitting in the participant list either, especially when the conversation may include private product or business details. keeping the recording, transcription, translation, and summary entirely on the Mac feels like the right trust model.

The one-time price is refreshing too. Curious how QuickQuill reliably captures both sides of a call across different apps without installing a virtual audio driver, and how well speaker separation works when several people are talking?

Report

Messaging apps [...] The main trade-off is that voice-centric work isn’t ideal for every culture or timezone mix, but for teams that thrive on real-time interaction, Quill provides a distinctive collaboration style that Slack doesn’t prioritize.

### Best for

Best for distributed teams that want casual, always-on voice collaboration alongside chat.

### Standout features

 ✓Persistent voice channels for ambient teamwork
 ✓Lightweight drop-in conversations
 ✓Messaging paired with voice presence
 ✓Designed for spontaneous team interaction

## SoWork

SoWorkSoWork

All-in-one virtual HQ for high-performing remote teams

4.7 · 25 reviews

Learn more → [...] For distributed teams, that “always available” voice presence can unlock faster alignment and more spontaneous problem-solving. It’s a strong alternative when the goal is recreating informal social glue—quick questions, casual check-ins, or a shared sense of being together—without spinning up a formal call.

Compared with Slack’s typical text-first flow, Quill’s voice-first environment can reduce the friction of jumping into conversations and help teams keep momentum. It’s particularly useful for teams that find that chat alone makes collaboration feel transactional.

RocketChat

RocketChatThe ultimate chat platform

5.0 (4 reviews)

Team collaboration software

•

Messaging apps

•

Slack apps [...] A key differentiator is its always-available voice channels, which create a lightweight “virtual office” feel. Instead of scheduling formal calls, teams can drop into voice spaces for quick questions, pairing sessions, or casual check-ins.

By making active voice channels visible and easy to join, Quill encourages spontaneous interaction without the overhead of video meetings. This is especially compelling for distributed teams that want to recreate the ambient presence of being co-located.

The trade-off is that Quill is purpose-built for work collaboration rather than broad consumer messaging and large public communities. If your priority is focused team communication plus frictionless voice, Quill is a more direct fit than Telegram 4.7.
